description: Following the flowers, the plant produces shiny berries that are initially green. They gradually become a glossy bright red from September to December.  They usually remain on the plant until the plant flowers the following year.

care.soil_types: Peat or soil with specific nutrients
care.fertilizer_type: Dilute fertilizers following instructions, apply 1-2 times monthly
care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day) | Climate: subtropical
care.tips: Remove aged tissues, dead and pest branches timely | Similar plants: Ardisia japonica | Native to: NSW, IND GO, EASTERN ASIA, HAW JI, PHI OO
common_problems: No serious insect or disease problems

This plant is considered invasive in parts of the southern United States, southern Africa (including South Africa, Madagascar, Mauritius, Réunion, and the Seychelles), and Oceania (including the Cook Islands, Fiji, and New Zealand)
Check with local laws and recommendations before adding this plant to the landscape in these areas

Uses

Where not invasive, group in shrub borders, shade gardens or woodland areas
May be grown as a houseplant in containers t

description: Ardisia elliptica, commonly known as shoebutton ardisia or duck’s-eyes, is a tropical to semi-tropical broadleaf evergreen shrub or small tree that typically grows in understory thickets to 10-16’ tall and as wide. It is native to the western coast of India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Southeast Asia, New Guinea and the Philippines, but has been widely introduced and cultivated as an ornamental in warm locations throughout the world, with naturalization occurring in such places as Puerto Rico, some Car

care.watering_guide: Missouri BG: Winter hardy to USDA Zones 8-10 where it is easily grown in moist, acidic, organically rich soils in part shade to full shade. It thrives in moderately wet soil conditions. Foliage may burn in full sun locations. Often spreads aggressively in the landscape by self-seeding.
care.tips: Native to: MLY, WIN GR, SUL, ASIA TROPICAL, NORTHERN SOUTH AMERICA
common_problems: No serious insect or disease problems

Uses

Invasive plant that cannot legally be grown in areas where growth is prohibited by law
In areas where it may be legally grown, growth is often discouraged where aggressive spread is likely to occur
This plant generally performs best in shady areas with moist to wet soils
Often thrives in moist woodland areas and shade gardens

description: The fruits appear in September and mature from October to November. They may remain on the shrub throughout the winter. They are bright red drupes and measure 0.25 inches in diameter. They have a fleshy outer covering with a hardened pit or stone in the center that contains the seed.

care.soil_types: Soil with loose texture or soil with specific nutrients
care.fertilizer_type: Dilute fertilizers following instructions, apply 1-2 times monthly

Uses

Ground cover shrub for shrub borders, shade gardens or woodland areas

description: The fruit is a small, round capsule that has 5 to 7 chambers. The capsule will mature during the plant's dormancy and then split open to deposit the seeds. The seeds usually ripen in early to mid-summer.

care.light_guide: NC State: Partial Shade (Direct sunlight only part of the day, 2-6 hours)
common_problems: No significant insect or disease problems.
The corms are intolerant of poorly drained soils, especially during summer dormancy.
Mice and squirrels may feed on the corms.
This plant is toxic to humans, cats, dogs, and horses if ingested.
Keep this plant out of the reach of children and pets.
common_pests: No significant insect or disease problems.

description: The fruit is a capsule that measures about 0.5 inches in diameter and is divided into five chambers. The capsule contains 5-40 sticky, sugary seeds. When the seeds are ripening, the capsule will open. Ants are reportedly attracted to the seeds.

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day)

description: Hottonia palustris, also water violet or featherfoil, is an aquatic plant in the family Primulaceae.

Lysimachia arvensis, syn. Anagallis arvensis, commonly known as scarlet pimpernel, red pimpernel, red chickweed, poor man's barometer, poor man's weather-glass, shepherd's weather glass or shepherd's clock, is a species of low-growing annual plant with brightly coloured flowers, most often scarlet but also bright blue and sometimes pink. The native range of the species is Europe and Western Asia and North Africa.

Lysimachia europaea is a flowering plant in the primrose family Primulaceae, called by the common name chickweed-wintergreen or arctic starflower. It is a small herbaceous perennial plant with one or more whorls of leaves on a single slender erect stem. It is about 10 cm, 3.9 in high. The broad lanceolate leaves are pale green but take on a copper hue in late summer. The solitary white flowers are reminiscent of small wood anemones and appear in midsummer.

Lysimachia maritima is a plant species belonging to the family Primulaceae. It was previously called Glaux maritima, the only species in the monotypic genus Glaux. The species has a number of common names, including sea milkwort, sea milkweed, and black saltwort.
Lysimachia nemorum, the yellow pimpernel, is a perennial flowering plant in the family Primulaceae.
Lysimachia nummularia is a species of flowering plant in the primrose family Primulaceae. Its common names include moneywort, creeping jenny, herb twopence and twopenny grass.

Lysimachia thyrsiflora, the tufted loosestrife, is a plant in the genus Lysimachia. It is native to large sections of the northern Northern Hemisphere, including Eurasia and North America. It often grows in marshes, shorelines of lakes and ponds and occasionally along streams. It is an erect perennial herb growing up to 80 centimeters (31 in) tall and bearing yellow flowers, sometimes dotted with purple.

Lysimachia vulgaris, the yellow loosestrife or garden loosestrife, is a species of herbaceous perennial flowering plant in the family Primulaceae. It was transferred to Myrsinoideae based on results of molecular phylogenetic research before being merged into the Primulaceae.

Primula elatior, the oxlip, is a species of flowering plant in the family Primulaceae, native to nutrient-poor and calcium-rich damp woods and meadows throughout Europe, with northern borders in Denmark and southern parts of Sweden, eastwards to the Altai Mountains and on the Kola Peninsula in Russia, and westwards in the British Isles.

Primula farinosa, the bird's-eye primrose, is a small perennial plant in the family Primulaceae, native to Northern Europe and further south at high altitudes in the mountains of southern Europe. It is also widespread in northern and central Asia. This primrose thrives on grazed meadows rich in lime and moisture.

Primula veris, the cowslip, common cowslip, or cowslip primrose, is a herbaceous perennial flowering plant in the primrose family Primulaceae. The species is native throughout most of temperate Europe and western Asia, and although absent from more northerly areas including much of northwest Scotland, it reappears in northernmost Sutherland and Orkney and in Scandinavia.

Primula vulgaris is a species of flowering plant in the family Primulaceae, native to Eurasia. The common name of this plant is primrose, or occasionally common primrose or English primrose to distinguish it from other Primula species referred to as primroses.

Samolus is a widely distributed genus of about a dozen species of water-loving herbs and amphibious flowering plants. According to the APG III system of classification, the genus falls within the primrose family, Primulaceae, under the order containing the rhododendroid eudicots, Ericales. It was considered closely related to a clade comprising the Theophrastaceae, and was likewise treated as part of that family.
